Artistic movement; German equivalent of Art Nouveau

| --- | --- |
| Top: Ernst Ludwig House at the [Darmstadt Artists' Colony](/wiki/Darmstadt_Artists%27_Colony "Darmstadt Artists' Colony"); Center: cover of [Jugend](/wiki/Jugend_(magazine) "Jugend (magazine)") magazine by [Otto Eckmann](/wiki/Otto_Eckmann "Otto Eckmann") (1896); Bottom: Dining room of [Peter Behrens](/wiki/Peter_Behrens "Peter Behrens") in Darmstadt (1900–1901) | |

***Jugendstil*** (German pronunciation: [[ˈjuːɡn̩tˌstiːl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Jugendstil.ogg "File:De-Jugendstil.ogg"); "Youth Style") was an artistic movement, particularly in the [decorative arts](/wiki/Decorative_arts "Decorative arts"), that was influential primarily in Germany, Austria, and elsewhere in Europe to a lesser extent from about 1895 until about 1910.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) It was the German and Austrian counterpart of [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau"). The members of the movement were reacting against the [historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)") and [neo-classicism](/wiki/Neo-classicism "Neo-classicism") of the official art and architecture academies. It took its name from the art journal *[Jugend](/wiki/Jugend_(magazine) "Jugend (magazine)")*, founded by the German artist [Georg Hirth](/wiki/Georg_Hirth "Georg Hirth"). It was especially active in the graphic arts and interior decoration.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Its major centers of activity were [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar") and the [Darmstadt Artists' Colony](/wiki/Darmstadt_Artists%27_Colony "Darmstadt Artists' Colony") founded in [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt") in 1901. Important figures of the movement included the Swiss graphic artist [Hermann Obrist](/wiki/Hermann_Obrist "Hermann Obrist"), [Otto Eckmann](/wiki/Otto_Eckmann "Otto Eckmann"), the Belgian architect and decorator [Henry van de Velde](/wiki/Henry_van_de_Velde "Henry van de Velde"), as well as the Austrians [Otto Wagner](/wiki/Otto_Wagner "Otto Wagner"), [Joseph Maria Olbrich](/wiki/Joseph_Maria_Olbrich "Joseph Maria Olbrich"), [Gustav Klimt](/wiki/Gustav_Klimt "Gustav Klimt"), and [Koloman Moser](/wiki/Koloman_Moser "Koloman Moser"), among others. In its earlier years, the style was influenced by the British [Modern Style](/wiki/Modern_Style_(British_Art_Nouveau_style) "Modern Style (British Art Nouveau style)"). It was also influenced by Japanese prints. Later, under the [Secessionists](/wiki/Secession_(art) "Secession (art)")' influence, it tended toward abstraction and more geometrical forms.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)

From 1898 to 1903, The Vienna Secession, led by [Gustav Klimt](/wiki/Gustav_Klimt "Gustav Klimt") and [Max Kurzweil](/wiki/Max_Kurzweil "Max Kurzweil") published the journal [Ver Sacrum (magazine)](/wiki/Ver_Sacrum_(magazine) "Ver Sacrum (magazine)") , an important chronicle of many of the groups artistic contributions to the world of art and design.

The [Secession Building](/wiki/Secession_Building "Secession Building"), completed in 1898 by [Joseph Maria Olbrich](/wiki/Joseph_Maria_Olbrich "Joseph Maria Olbrich") in Vienna, is widely regarded as one of Europes most noteworthy early modernist buildings in the style of the Vienna Secession.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The movement had its origins in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") with the founding of an association of visual artists in 1892, which broke away from the more formal historical and academic styles of the academy. [Georg Hirth](/wiki/Georg_Hirth "Georg Hirth") chose the name [Munich Secession](/wiki/Munich_Secession "Munich Secession") for the association. Later, the [Vienna Secession](/wiki/Vienna_Secession "Vienna Secession"), founded in 1897 and the [Berlin Secession](/wiki/Berlin_Secession "Berlin Secession") took their own names from the Munich group. The journal of the group, *[Jugend](/wiki/Jugend_(magazine) "Jugend (magazine)")*, begun in 1896, along with another Munich publication, *[Simplicissimus](/wiki/Simplicissimus "Simplicissimus")* and *[Pan](/wiki/Pan_(magazine) "Pan (magazine)")* in Berlin, became the most visible showcases of the new style. The leading figures of this movement, including [Peter Behrens](/wiki/Peter_Behrens "Peter Behrens"), [Bernhard Pankok](/wiki/Bernhard_Pankok "Bernhard Pankok"), and [Richard Riemerschmid](/wiki/Richard_Riemerschmid "Richard Riemerschmid"), as well as the majority of the founding members of the Munich Secession, all provided illustrations to *Jugend*.

In the beginning, the style was used primarily in illustrations and graphic arts. *Jugendstil* combined floral decoration and sinuous curves with more geometric lines, and soon was used for covers of novels, advertisements, and exhibition posters. Designers often created original styles of typeface that worked harmoniously with the image, such as the [Arnold Böcklin typeface](/wiki/Arnold_B%C3%B6cklin_(typeface) "Arnold Böcklin (typeface)") created in 1904.

[Otto Eckmann](/wiki/Otto_Eckmann "Otto Eckmann") was one of the most prominent German artists associated with both *Jugend* and *Pan*. His favourite animal was the swan, and so great was his influence that the swan came to serve as the symbol of the entire movement. Another prominent designer in the style was [Richard Riemerschmid](/wiki/Richard_Riemerschmid "Richard Riemerschmid"), who made furniture, pottery, and other decorative objects in a sober, geometric style that pointed forward toward [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco"). The Swiss artist [Hermann Obrist](/wiki/Hermann_Obrist "Hermann Obrist"), living in Munich, made designs with sinuous double curves, modeled after plants and flowers, which were a prominent motif of the early style.

### Joseph Maria Olbrich and the Darmstadt Artists' Colony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Joseph Maria Olbrich and the Darmstadt Artists' Colony")]

The [Darmstadt Artists' Colony](/wiki/Darmstadt_Artists%27_Colony "Darmstadt Artists' Colony") is a remarkable collection of *Jugendstil* buildings created beginning in 1899 by [Ernest Ludwig, Grand Duke of Hesse](/wiki/Ernst_Ludwig,_Grand_Duke_of_Hesse "Ernst Ludwig, Grand Duke of Hesse"), a grandson of Queen Victoria, to promote both commerce and the arts. He brought together a group of designers to create his new community, including [Peter Behrens](/wiki/Peter_Behrens "Peter Behrens"), [Hans Christiansen](/wiki/Hans_Christiansen_(artist) "Hans Christiansen (artist)"), and [Joseph Maria Olbrich](/wiki/Joseph_Maria_Olbrich "Joseph Maria Olbrich").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Colony architecture represented a complete break with the earlier floral style, and was much bolder in its design. Behrens and several of the other architects built their own houses there, and designed every detail, from the doorknobs to the dishes.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The most impressive building of the Colony is the Ernst-Ludwig House, named for the Grand Duke, which contained the workshops of the artists. It was designed by Olbrich, with an entrance in the form of a three-quarter circle, flanked by two statues, *Force* and *Beauty*, by Ludwig Habich (1901).

### Ålesund

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ålesund")]

The Norwegian town of [Ålesund](/wiki/%C3%85lesund "Ålesund") suffered a disastrous fire[[5]](#cite_note-5) on 23 January 1904. With the support of [Kaiser Wilhelm II of Germany](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany "Kaiser Wilhelm II of Germany") the town was reconstructed in Jugendstil by local Norwegian designers and architects.[[6]](#cite_note-6) To honor Wilhelm, one of the most frequented streets[[7]](#cite_note-7) of the town is named after him.

The city of [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar") was another important center of the *Jugendstil*, thanks largely to the Belgian architect and designer [Henry van de Velde](/wiki/Henry_van_de_Velde "Henry van de Velde"). Van de Velde had played an important role in the early Belgian Art Nouveau, building his own house and decorating it in Art Nouveau style, with the strong influence of the British [Arts and Crafts Movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_Movement "Arts and Crafts Movement"). He was a known in Germany for his work in Belgium and Paris, and began a new career in Dresden in 1897, with a display at the Dresden Exposition of decorative arts. His work became known in Germany through decorative arts journals, and he received several commissions for interiors in Berlin, for the [Villa Esche](/wiki/Villa_Esche "Villa Esche") in [Chemnitz](/wiki/Chemnitz "Chemnitz"), the [Folkwang Museum](/wiki/Folkwang_Museum "Folkwang Museum") in [Hagen](/wiki/Hagen "Hagen"), and the Nietzsche House in [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar") for [Elisabeth Förster-Nietzsche](/wiki/Elisabeth_F%C3%B6rster-Nietzsche "Elisabeth Förster-Nietzsche"), the sister of philosopher [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche"). He settled in Weimar in 1899 and produced a wide variety of decorative works, including silverware and ceramics, all in strikingly original forms. His silverware was particularly unusual: each piece had its own form, with sleek curving lines, but together they formed a harmonious ensemble. In 1902, he decorated the apartment of Count [Harry Kessler](/wiki/Harry_Graf_Kessler "Harry Graf Kessler"), a prominent patron of the Impressionist painters.[[8]](#cite_note-Sembach_1991,_pp._132–134-8)

In 1905, with the patronage of the Grand Duke of Weimar, he created the Grand Ducal School of Arts and Crafts in Weimar. He created a showcase of applied arts for the Dresden Exposition of Applied Arts in 1906, decorated with paintings by [Ludwig von Hofmann](/wiki/Ludwig_von_Hofmann "Ludwig von Hofmann"), intended as the main room of a new museum of decoration in Weimar. He transposed the characteristics of his silverware, dishes, and furniture into the architecture. Van de Velde left off the curling vegetal lines of Art Nouveau decoration and replaced them with much simpler, more stylized curves which were part of the structure of his buildings and decorative works.[[8]](#cite_note-Sembach_1991,_pp._132–134-8)

The importance of Weimar as a cultural center of the *Jugendstil* was ended in 1906, when its main patron, Count Harry Kessler, commissioned [Rodin](/wiki/Rodin "Rodin") to make a nude statue for the Grand Duke. The Grand Duke was scandalized, and Kessler was forced to resign. The Weimar school of design lost its importance until 1919, when it returned as the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") under [Walter Gropius](/wiki/Walter_Gropius "Walter Gropius"), and played a major part in the emergence of [modern architecture](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Peter Behrens and the German *Werkbund*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Peter Behrens and the German Werkbund")]

The architect and designer [Peter Behrens](/wiki/Peter_Behrens "Peter Behrens") (1868–1940) was a key figure in the final years of the *Jugendstil*, and in the transition to modern architecture. Born in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), where he studied painting, Behrens moved to Munich in 1890 and worked as a painter, illustrator and bookbinder. In 1890, he was one of the founders of the [Munich Secession](/wiki/Munich_Secession "Munich Secession"). In 1899, he was invited to participate in the [Darmstadt Artists' Colony](/wiki/Darmstadt_Artists%27_Colony "Darmstadt Artists' Colony"), where he designed his own house and all of its contents, including the furniture, towels and dishes.

After 1900 he became involved in [industrial design](/wiki/Industrial_design "Industrial design") and the reform of architecture to more functional forms. In 1902, he participated in the Turin International Exposition, one of the first major Europe-wide showcases of Art Nouveau. In 1907, Behrens and a group of other notable *Jugendstil* artists, including [Hermann Muthesius](/wiki/Hermann_Muthesius "Hermann Muthesius"), [Theodor Fischer](/wiki/Theodor_Fischer "Theodor Fischer"), [Josef Hoffmann](/wiki/Josef_Hoffmann "Josef Hoffmann"), [Joseph Maria Olbrich](/wiki/Joseph_Maria_Olbrich "Joseph Maria Olbrich"), [Bruno Paul](/wiki/Bruno_Paul "Bruno Paul"), [Richard Riemerschmid](/wiki/Richard_Riemerschmid "Richard Riemerschmid"), and [Fritz Schumacher](/wiki/Fritz_Schumacher_(architect) "Fritz Schumacher (architect)"), created the [Deutscher Werkbund](/wiki/Deutscher_Werkbund "Deutscher Werkbund"). Modeled after the [Arts and Crafts movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_movement "Arts and Crafts movement") in England, its goal was to improve and modernize the design of industrial products and everyday objects. He first major project was [AEG turbine factory](/wiki/AEG_turbine_factory "AEG turbine factory") in Berlin (1908–1909). Behren's assistants and students at this time included [Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Mies_van_der_Rohe "Mies van der Rohe"), C. E. Jeanerette (the future [Le Corbusier](/wiki/Le_Corbusier "Le Corbusier")), and [Walter Gropius](/wiki/Walter_Gropius "Walter Gropius"), the future head of the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus"). The work of Behrens and the Werkbund effectively launched the transition from the *Jugendstil* to modernism in Germany, and the end of the *Jugendstil*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Architecture and decoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Architecture and decoration")]

In Berlin, [August Endell](/wiki/August_Endell "August Endell") was both editor of *[Pan](/wiki/Pan_(magazine) "Pan (magazine)")* magazine and a major figure in *Jugendstil* decoration, designing hotels and theaters, such as the interior of Buntes Theater in Berlin (1901), destroyed during World War II. He designed every detail of the interior down to the nails. with each room in a different color, and on a different theme. He also designed the [Hackesche Höfe](/wiki/Hackesche_H%C3%B6fe "Hackesche Höfe"), a complex of buildings in the centre of Berlin, noted for the imaginative details of the decoration, in spirals and curling forms,

## Posters and graphic arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Posters and graphic arts")]

Main article: [Art Nouveau posters and graphic arts](/wiki/Art_Nouveau_posters_and_graphic_arts "Art Nouveau posters and graphic arts")

The most prominent graphic artist was [Otto Eckmann](/wiki/Otto_Eckmann "Otto Eckmann"), who produced numerous illustrations for the movement's journal *[Jugend](/wiki/Jugend_(magazine) "Jugend (magazine)")*, in a sinuous, floral style that was similar to the French style. He also created a type style based upon Japanese calligraphy. [Joseph Sattler](/wiki/Joseph_Sattler "Joseph Sattler") was another graphic artist who contributed to the style through another artistic journal called *[Pan](/wiki/Pan_(magazine) "Pan (magazine)")*. Sattler designed a type face often used in *Jugendstil*.

Another important German graphic artist was Josef Rudolf Witzel (1867–1925), who produced many early covers for *Jugend*, with curving, floral forms which helped shape the style.

The magazine *[Simplicissimus](/wiki/Simplicissimus "Simplicissimus")*, published in Munich, was also noted for its *Jugendstil* graphics, as well as for the modern writers it presented, including [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann") and [Rainer Maria Rilke](/wiki/Rainer_Maria_Rilke "Rainer Maria Rilke"). Important illustrators for the magazine included [Thomas Theodor Heine](/wiki/Thomas_Theodor_Heine "Thomas Theodor Heine").

## Furniture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Furniture")]

The ideal of designers of the *Jugendstil* was to make a house a complete work of art, with everything inside, from the furniture to the carpets and the dishware, silverware and the art, in perfect harmony. With this ideal in mind, they established their own workshops to produce furniture.

[August Endell](/wiki/August_Endell "August Endell"), [Theodor Fischer](/wiki/Theodor_Fischer "Theodor Fischer"), [Bruno Paul](/wiki/Bruno_Paul "Bruno Paul"), and especially [Richard Riemerschmid](/wiki/Richard_Riemerschmid "Richard Riemerschmid") were important figures in *Jugendstil* furniture.

## Metalware

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jugendstil&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Metalware")]

[Metallwarenfabrik Straub & Schweizer](/wiki/WMF_Group "WMF Group") (WMF) was, by 1900, the world's largest producer of household metalware, mainly in the *Jugendstil* style, designed in the WMF Art Studio under Albert Mayer. WMF purchased Orivit, another company known for its *Jugendstil* pewter, in 1905.
